text: More serious than the usual build-a-dApp tutorials and it speaks Java: web3j, BigInteger, ABI. But the framing is openly for Swiss in-house core-banking developers — CHF, Rappen, SIC. I'm a delivery guy in Bengaluru shipping back-ends for clients, so I'm adjacent but not the target; it assumes I operate a core system when I build modules to spec.

This clicked hard. EVM account as key pair, msg.sender as authenticated Principal baked into the protocol, gas as a metered batch quota, revert as ROLLBACK that still costs — gold for a Spring dev. One confusion: it keeps saying you already operate HSMs and EOD batches in production; I integrate with those but never owned them, so the you-do-this-daily tone overshoots my reality.

Strong chapter. ERC-20 as a securities register finally made tokens make sense: the balances mapping is the positions table, Transfer is the journal entry, approve is power of attorney. The single booking-engine pattern with checked/unchecked is genuinely well taught. Only nit: everything is CHF, an INR example would connect faster.

This is my chapter, the one I'd use at work, and it's the weakest by a mile. EthFilter passes a contract name string where an address is required and won't work, it imports a non-existent FunctionDecoder, several classes are used but never imported, and the nonce, gas and idempotency steps just paste the identical method body with a renamed method. Idempotency is an in-memory HashSet lost on restart. As the most relevant chapter to my job, a real letdown.

The title assumes I came from core banking, but I came from QA and two years of Spring. The prerequisites literally say core-banking concepts like ledgers, EOD batch and HSMs are assumed. I don't know what an HSM or EOD batch is, so the first line tells me this might not be for me. The Java analogies are comforting but tangled with Swiss banking words I have to guess.

The Java to EVM mappings helped a lot and gave me small wins. What lost me: settles in batches, EOD batch, HSM-signed messages, ISO 20022, secp256k1, Keccak, last 20 bytes — dropped as if obvious. I'm shaky on bytes and have zero crypto, so hash it and keep the last 20 bytes is hand-wavy magic; I'd need it slower.

The strongest chapter for me because the code builds step by step, and balances mapping equals the register clicked. But the frame is finance idioms I don't have: registrar, transfer agent, coupon, par, corporate action. The unchecked blocks scared me because overflow is my weak spot and trust the invariant needs me to see something I can't yet. I had to read the approve race three times.

This felt thinner and lower quality than ch01 and ch04. The filter uses the literal string CmtatToken where a real address should go, which looks like a bug. The nonce handling step is identical code to the previous step with nothing added, and idempotency is just an in-memory HashSet lost on restart, which contradicts the chapter that said the chain is the dedupe store. I'd get stuck trusting it.

Promising. The analogical scaffolding — ERC-20 as securities register, Transfer as booking entry, approve as power of attorney — is exactly what I need and it's not marketing fluff: every step has validation rules. The dev-only stand-in for the HSM caveat shows the author thinks about how this lands in a bank. Respects my time.

Right depth, occasionally great. The BigInteger warning, EIP-55 checksum as IBAN check digit, revert-still-pays, finalized vs latest as settlement finality, nonce as SWIFT sequence — correct production details I expect from a senior. Minor hand-waving: finality not quantified as a confirmation-depth policy, and block.timestamp manipulation mentioned without its ordering consequences. Solid foundation.

Strong. The single-chokepoint update model with each unchecked block justified by a named invariant, decimals zero for indivisible shares, setScale without rounding to force a throw — that is the rigor I want. The approve race is explained correctly with a real ops mitigation, and the idempotent mint via topic filter closes the reconciliation loop. The best chapter.

This is where it falls apart, and it is the chapter that matters most to me. It promises nonce management, gas strategy, idempotency, reorg handling and delivers none. EthFilter passes the literal string CmtatToken as an address in every example and won't compile. Nonce conflicts and gas strategy are byte-identical copies of the basic submit. Idempotency is an in-memory HashSet wiped on restart. Reorg handling just checks a receipt is present once. The audit trail stores only a hash, discarding data. It reads machine-generated and was never run.

Mapping is exactly how I onboard onto a client domain: this new thing is the thing you already build. That framing lets me bill confidently because I'm translating, not learning from zero. The Swiss in-house framing isn't a blocker for a consultant; the analogies are generic enough to reuse with any financial-services client.

Strong. Every concept has a concrete anchor: msg.sender as authenticated Principal, revert as ROLLBACK, finality as settlement finality. The Credentials.create equals dev-only stand-in for the HSM callout stops a junior shipping a key to prod. The datatype notes, uint256 to BigInteger never long, are real bugs I'd otherwise hit. Useful even though it is just Solidity.

Best chapter for me. ERC-20 is a securities register reframes the whole thing into terms I can hand a client. The approve power-of-attorney plus the front-running set-to-zero caveat is exactly the gotcha that bites in delivery. The lossless BigDecimal base-units round trip and operationId as end-to-end idempotency key are directly billable adapter code.

Disappointing, and it's the one I came for. The event filter passes the literal string CmtatToken where a contract address must go. Nonce conflict handling and gas strategy are copy-paste duplicates of the basic submit. Idempotency is an in-memory HashSet that evaporates on restart, useless for a bank adapter and contradicting ch04. The audit trail just hashes a string. And Credentials.create inline violates ch01's own HSM warning.

Looks like learn-by-doing on paper: every step has instruction, starter, solution, validation, so I can type and get a check. But the wall of banking prose before each editor box is heavy; three to five paragraphs per step before I touch code. I skim that and jump to starter code, so a lot is wasted on me.

Doing density is fine, real starter and solution to fill in. But the validation bites: the inline rule text and the actual checker regex don't always match, and a checker that contradicts its own description is the worst failure mode for someone who learns by feedback. Content-wise, msg.sender as authenticated Principal and gas as a batch quota landed, that is how I think.

The strong one. Fill-in-the-blank starters force me to type the real thing instead of copying a whole solution. The update engine built once clicked. But the CS-theory gaps hurt: unchecked, overflow, type max as unlimited assume two's-complement wraparound I don't really know, and there's no step where I trigger an overflow and watch it revert. I'd believe it more if I could break it.

This is where I'd quit. After the craftsmanship of ch04, ch08 is broken and lazy: it subscribes to a contract literally named CmtatToken as the address, which won't run, and the checker requires that broken string. The nonce, gas and idempotency lessons are the same copy-pasted body with nothing added. The audit trail is a SHA-256 of a string with no chaining. For a learn-by-doing dev, code that doesn't compile and checks that pass on stubs means zero trust.
